candidate_problems.text: What concrete structural criteria distinguish genuine self-correction of goals from mere higher-order optimization of fixed goals? The conjecture hinges on 'revisable causes' and 'criticism' as safety-relevant capacities, but it leaves open how to operationalize these notions so they are not trivially attributed to any sufficiently complex controller or denied to all systems. This is a key open problem because without a non-circular criterion, the view cannot guide evaluation or design of agents.

How can goal-revision remain safe and coherent without collapsing into arbitrariness, reward hacking, or loss of identity? The conjecture treats revisability as the core of meaningful freedom and safety, but does not resolve the tension between corrigibility and stability: a system must be able to criticize its aims, yet unrestricted revision may destroy long-term reliability or make the agent manipulable. The missing theory is what constraints make self-modification truth-tracking rather than destabilizing.

Are reasons and world-model criticism genuinely causally explanatory at the right level, or are they only convenient redescriptions of lower-level dynamics? The conjecture asserts that reasons can be real causes when instantiated in systems capable of acting on them, but this raises an unresolved philosophical and scientific question about causal autonomy across levels. Clarifying when higher-level epistemic states have explanatory and intervention-relevant status is important if the framework is to support claims about agency and AI safety rather than merely redescribe deterministic processes.
